{{Infobox 学者 | name = 白石 太一郎 | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place ={{JPN}}[[大阪府]][[大阪市]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = [[考古学]] |alma_mater = [[同志社大学]] |workplaces = [[古代学協会]]、[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所]]、[[国立歴史民俗博物館]]([[総合研究大学院大学]])、[[奈良大学]]、[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]] |degree = <!--学位--> | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }} '''白石 太一郎'''（しらいし たいちろう、[[1938年]][[11月14日]]<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref> - ）は、[[日本]]の[[考古学者]]。[[国立歴史民俗博物館]]名誉教授、[[総合研究大学院大学]]名誉教授。[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]名誉館長。
== 経歴 == 1938年、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]]に生まれた。[[大阪星光学院中学校・高等学校|大阪星光学院高等学校]]を経て、1961年[[同志社大学文学部]]文化学科文化史専攻を卒業。同大学院文学研究科文化史専攻に進み、1964年に[[大学院#修士課程・博士前期課程|修士課程]]修了、1968年に同[[大学院#博士後期課程・後期3年博士課程|博士課程]]を[[単位取得満期退学]]した。
1986年4月より[[古代学協会]]研究員に就いた。翌1969年8月より[[奈良県立橿原考古学研究所]]に勤務。1978年に[[文化庁]]国立歴史民俗博物館設立準備室調査官となり、[[国立歴史民俗博物館]]の設立準備にあたった。1981年に同館が開館すると、同館助教授に就いた。1984年に教授([[総合研究大学院大学]]教授)昇格。同副館長を務めた。2004年、国立歴史民俗博物館(総合研究大学院大学)を定年退職し、名誉教授となった。2004年4月からは[[奈良大学]]文学部教授として教鞭をとった。2009年に奈良大学を定年退職。その後は[[大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館]]館長<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[百舌鳥・古市古墳群]]、[[世界遺産]]の価値十分」(産経新聞)]</ref>。
[[放送大学]]客員教授ほか多数の大学で客員教授を兼任し、文化庁[[国宝]][[高松塚古墳|高松塚壁画]]恒久保存対策検討会委員など遺跡や文化財保存に関わる委員を務めた。
==受賞・栄典== * 2002年：[[雄山閣]]考古学賞を受賞。 * 2020年：[[地域文化功労者]]表彰<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2年度地域文化功労者表彰名簿]</ref>。
==研究内容・業績== 専門は日本[[考古学]]で日本の古代国家・古代文化形成過程の解明を目指している。
; 見解 * [[邪馬台国畿内説]]支持派<!-- 『倭国誕生』において、考古学研究の成果から考えて、現状では畿内説が有力としている -->。 *邪馬台国の時代の[[土器]]の移動に関する研究から[[神武東征]]伝説については、史実ではなかった可能性を指摘している。<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002年 ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * [[箸墓古墳]]の被葬者を[[卑弥呼]]に比定し、[[古墳]]が造られた年代順から天理市の[[西殿塚古墳]]を[[台与]]の墓であると考察している。<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004年 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== 著作 == ; 著書 * 『墳丘と内部構造』(古墳の知識 1) [[東京美術]]・考古学シリーズ 1985 * 『[[古墳時代|古墳の造られた時代]]』(日本のあけぼの 5) [[毎日新聞社]] 1989 * 『古墳の語る古代史』歴史民俗博物館振興会・歴博ブックレット 1998 **[[岩波現代文庫]] 2000 * 『古墳と[[ヤマト政権]] 古代国家はいかに形成されたか』[[文藝春秋]]<[[文春新書]]> 1999 * 『古墳と古墳群の研究』塙書房 2000 * 『古墳とその時代』[[山川出版社]]・日本史リブレット 2001 * 『考古学と古代史の間』[[筑摩書房]]・ちくまプリマーブックス 2004 **[[ちくま学芸文庫]] 2009 * 『近畿の古墳と古代史』[[学生社]] 2007 **[[吉川弘文館]]<読みなおす日本史>[https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b608823.html] 2022 ISBN 9784642075145 * 『東国の古墳と古代史』学生社 2007 **吉川弘文館<読みなおす日本史> [https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b608835.html] 2022 ISBN 9784642075152 * 『考古学からみた倭国』[[青木書店]] 2009 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』塙書房 2011 * 『古墳からみた倭国の形成と展開』敬文舎・日本歴史私の最新講義 2013 * 『古墳の被葬者を推理する』[[中央公論新社]]<[[中公叢書]]> 2018
; 共編著 * 『古墳 古代を考える』[https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b32321.html]編 [[吉川弘文館]] 1989 ISBN 9784642021470 * 『終末期古墳と古代国家 古代を考える』[https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b32360.html]編 吉川弘文館 2005 ISBN 9784642021944 * 『古墳時代の工芸』(古代史復元 7) 編 [[講談社]] 1990 * 『古代編 1 古墳～[[飛鳥時代]]』(争点日本の歴史 第2巻) [[吉村武彦]]共編 [[新人物往来社]] 1990 * 『古代編1 古墳～飛鳥時代』(新視点日本の歴史 第2巻) 吉村武彦共編 新人物往来社 1993 * 『[[藤ノ木古墳]] [[斑鳩]]に花開く東アジアの古代』(日本の古代遺跡を掘る 5) [[前園実知雄]]共著、[[読売新聞社]] 1995 * 『歴史考古学 発掘された飛鳥・奈良・平安時代』編著 [[放送大学教育振興会]] 1995 * 『シンポジウム日本の考古学 古墳時代の考古学』東潮・高木恭二・赤塚次郎共著、[[学生社]] 1998 * 『発掘された古代日本』編著 放送大学教育振興会 1999 * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』[https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b32041.html]編 吉川弘文館 2002 ISBN 9784642008013 * 『考古学と歴史』編 [[放送大学]]教材 2004 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』全8巻、[[上原真人]]・[[吉川真司]]・吉村武彦共編 [[岩波書店]] 2005-2006 {{colbegin|2}} #『古代史の舞台』 #『暮らしと生業』 #『社会集団と政治組織』 #『人と物の移動』 #『専門技能と技術』 #『[[言語]]と[[文字]]』 #『[[信仰]]と世界観』 #『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}} * 『終末期古墳と古代国家 古代を考える』編 吉川弘文館 2005 * 『[[邪馬台国]]からヤマト王権へ [[纒向遺跡]]の新発見が語るもの 講演会・シンポジウムの記録』 ::[[橋本輝彦]]・[[坂井秀弥]]共著 [[奈良大学]]ブックレット：[[ナカニシヤ出版]] 2010 * 『邪馬台(ヤマト)国 [[唐古・鍵遺跡]]から[[箸墓古墳]]へ』[[水野正好]]・西川寿勝共著、[[雄山閣]] 2010 **増補版 2015年 * 『天皇陵古墳を考える』編 [[今尾文昭]]・高橋照彦・[[森田克行]]共著、[[学生社]] 2012
; その他 * 『古墳と国家形成期の諸問題 白石太一郎先生傘寿記念論文集』同編集委員会編、[[山川出版社]] 2019

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). {{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.

revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' {{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|qq|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was appointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|qq|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was aqqointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohaqqyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohaqqyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|qq|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was aqqointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohaqqyo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[Osaka Prefecture]], [[Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[National Museum of Japanese History]] ({{ill|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|ja|総合研究大学院大学}}), [[Nara University]], [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|ja|高松塚古墳}}.
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
