revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.
== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.
== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[:en:The Open University of Japan|The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.
== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}
== External Links == * [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] Official Site

revision.text:
{{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[:en:The Open University of Japan|The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.

; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>

; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1938-11|birth_place={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|archaeologist|qid=Q3621491}}|given_name={{ill|Taichirō|qid=Q7675904}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11580156]] Wikidata name: Taichirō Shiraishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.

==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[:en:The Open University of Japan|The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.

== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1938-11|birth_place={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|archaeologist|qid=Q3621491}}|given_name={{ill|Taichirō|qid=Q7675904}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11580156]] Wikidata name: Taichirō Shiraishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
== References == {{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Pages with merged translatations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox scholar | name = Shirai Taichiro | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{JPN}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館|1=Heian Museum of Ancient History|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482389}}, [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[SOKENDAI]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Shirai Taichiro''' (白石 太一郎, born {{birth date|1938|11|14}}<ref>『読売年鑑 2016年版』（読売新聞東京本社、2016年）p.321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist. He is an emeritus professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[SOKENDAI]] and an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館|1=Heian Museum of Ancient History|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482389}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[:en:The Open University of Japan|The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.
== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}
== External Links == * [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] Official Site

== Biography == Born in Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture, in 1938, Shirai graduated from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin High School]] and proceeded to study at [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]], majoring in cultural history in the Faculty of Literature. He completed his master’s degree in 1964 and withdrew after fulfilling course requirements in the doctoral program in 1968.
In April 1968, Shirai joined the {{ill|Association of Ancient Studies|ja|古代学協会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Ancient Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平安博物館|1=Heian Museum of Ancient History|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482389}} as a researcher and subsequently began work at the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. He participated in preparations for the establishment of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] (Rekihaku) from 1978. Upon the museum's opening in 1981, he became an associate professor and was later promoted to professor in 1984. He also served as deputy director. In 2004, he retired from Rekihaku and SOKENDAI, becoming an emeritus professor.
From April 2004, Shirai served as a professor in the Faculty of Literature at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], retiring in 2009. He then took on the role of director at the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ 白石太一郎・大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館長「[[Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Group]]: Fully deserving of World Heritage status" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shirai has also held visiting professorships at many universities, including [[:en:The Open University of Japan|The Open University of Japan]], and served on committees for preserving historical sites and cultural properties, such as the committee for the preservation of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]] murals.
== Awards and Honors == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]] * 2020: Regional Culture Contributor Award<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 令和2nd year 度地域文化功労者表彰名簿 (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>.
== Research and Contributions == Shirai specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on elucidating the formation processes of ancient states and cultures in Japan.
; Views * Shirai su11orts the [[Yamatai-Koku Kinai Hypothesis]]. * His studies on the movement of pottery during the Yamatai period suggest that the [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] legend might not be based on historical events.<ref>日本時代史(1)『倭国誕生』白石太一郎編 2002nd year ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * Shirai has posited that the [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] could be the burial site of [[:en:Himiko|Himiko]], and that the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} may belong to [[:en:Toyo (queen)|Toyo]].<ref>『考古学と古代史の間』 2004th year 筑摩書房 ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Publications == ; Books * 『墳丘と内部構造』(Ancient Tomb Knowledge 1), [[Tokyo Bijutsu]], 1985 * 『古墳の造られた時代』, [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] Publishing, 1989 * 『古墳と古墳時代の文化』, Hanawa Shobo, 2011
; Edited Volumes * 『日本の時代史 倭国誕生』, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 2002 * 『列島の古代史 ひと・もの・こと』 (8 volumes), [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 2005–2006 {{colbegin|2}} # 『古代史の舞台』 # 『暮らしと生業』 # 『社会集団と政治組織』 # 『人と物の移動』 # 『専門技能と技術』 # 『言語と文字』 # 『信仰と世界観』 # 『古代史の流れ』 {{colend|2}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1938-11|birth_place={{ill|Osaka|qid=Q35765}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|archaeologist|qid=Q3621491}}|given_name={{ill|Taichirō|qid=Q7675904}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11580156]] Wikidata name: Taichirō Shiraishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox scholar | name = Taichiro Shiraishi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1938|11|14}} | birth_place = {{flagicon|Japan}} [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]] | death_date = | death_place = | othername = | main_interests = [[:en:Archaeology|Archaeology]] | alma_mater = [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] | workplaces = [[Ancient Studies Association]], [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]], [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] ([[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]), [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]], [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]] | degree = | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }}
'''Taichiro Shiraishi''' (白石 太一郎, ''Shiraishi Taichiro'', born November 14, 1938<ref>''Yomiuri Yearbook 2016'' (Yomiuri Shimbun Tokyo, 2016) p. 321</ref>) is a Japanese archaeologist and honorary professor at the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] and [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]. He is also an honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].
== Career == Shiraishi was born in Osaka City, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] in 1938. After graduating from [[Osaka Seiko Gakuin Junior and Senior High School|Osaka Seiko Academy High School]], he completed a degree in cultural history at the [[Doshisha University Faculty of Letters]] in 1961. He continued his studies in the graduate program at the same university, completing a master’s degree in 1964 before leaving the doctoral program without completing his degree in 1968.
He became a researcher at the [[Ancient Studies Association]] in April 1986 and began working at the [[Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] in August 1969. In 1978, he was a11ointed an investigator at the establishment preparation office of the [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]] under the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] to help establish the museum. When the museum opened in 1981, he became an associate professor. In 1984, he was promoted to professor (also serving as a professor at the [[:en:The Graduate University for Advanced Studies|The Graduate University for Advanced Studies]]) and later became the deputy director of the museum. He retired in 2004 and was named professor emeritus. Afterward, he served as a professor at the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]] until his retirement in 2009, later becoming the honorary director of the [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]].<ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20170726-7DXMKHCUYRJV3OCZFT7LW2GOHA/ Shiraishi Taichiro, Director of the Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum: "Mozu-Furuichi Kofun Cluster, World Heritage Value is Significant" (Sankei Shimbun)]</ref>
Shiraishi has also served as a visiting professor at the [[:en:Open University of Japan|Open University of Japan]] and other institutions and has held roles on cultural property preservation committees, such as the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]’ committee for the permanent preservation of the murals of the [[:en:Takamatsuzuka Tomb|Takamatsuzuka Tomb]].
== Awards == * 2002: Archaeology Prize from [[Yuzankaku Publishing]]. * 2020: Recognition for contributions to regional culture.<ref>[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodoha11yo/pdf/92732301_02.pdf 2020 Regional Cultural Contributors List]</ref>
== Research and Contributions == Shiraishi specializes in Japanese archaeology, focusing on the formation processes of ancient Japanese states and culture.
=== Views === * He is a proponent of the [[Yamatai Koku|Kinki Theory]] concerning the location of [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai]]. * Based on studies of pottery distribution during the Yamatai period, Shiraishi has suggested that the {{nihongo|Jinmu Expedition|神武東征|Jinmu tōsei}} legend may not be historical.<ref>Japanese History Volume 1: ''The Birth of Yamato,'' edited by Taichiro Shiraishi, 2002, ISBN 4-642-00801-2</ref> * He has proposed that the {{nihongo|Hashihaka Kofun|箸墓古墳}} was the tomb of [[Himiko]] and that the nearby {{nihongo|Nishimiya-no-zuka Kofun|西殿塚古墳|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun}} in Tenri City could be the tomb of [[Toyosukiirihime|Toyo]].<ref>''Between Archaeology and Ancient History,'' 2004, Chikuma Shobō, ISBN 4-480-04254-7</ref>
== Selected Publications == ; Books * ''Mound and Internal Structures'' (1st volume of ''Knowledge of Kofun'' series). Tokyo Bijutsu, 1985. * ''The Era of Kofun Construction'' (''Japan's Dawn'' Volume 5). Mainichi Newspapers, 1989. * ''Ancient History Told by Kofun.'' Rekihaku Booklet, 1998. ** Republished as part of [[Iwanami Modern Library]] in 2000. * ''Kofun and the Yamato Administration: How Ancient States Were Formed.'' [[:en:Bungeishunju|Bungeishunju]] (Bunshun Shinsho), 1999. * ''Research on Kofun and Kofun Clusters.'' Hanawa Shobo, 2000. * ''Kofun and Their Era.'' Yamakawa Publishing, Japanese History Booklet Series, 2001. * ''Between Archaeology and Ancient History.'' Chikuma Shobō, 2004. ** Republished in [[Chikuma Gakugei Library]], 2009. * ''The Kofun of Kinki and Ancient History.'' Student Publishing, 2007. * ''From Kofun to the Formation of Yamato.'' Aoki Shoten, 2009. * ''Who Were the Buried People of Kofun?'' Central Koron Shinsha, Chuo Koron Library Series, 2018.
